title: Tension Headache
oneLiner: A bilateral, pressing/tightening headache of mild-moderate intensity, not worsened by routine activity, caused by pericranial muscle tension and central sensitization.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 32-year-old female marketing executive presents with a bifrontal "band-like" headache that started mid-afternoon. She describes it as a constant pressure, not throbbing. No nausea, no photophobia, no phonophobia (or only one of these). She has been working long hours at a computer, and her neck feels stiff. Neurological exam is normal, and vital signs are stable. She reports similar headaches 2-3 times per month.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Bifrontal, "band-like" pain** → hallmark of tension-type headache (TTH); bilateral, pressing quality
- **Mid-afternoon onset** → often builds slowly as the day progresses, reflecting muscle tension accumulation
- **Constant pressure, not throbbing** → distinguishes from migraine (which is often pulsatile)
- **No nausea, no photophobia/phonophobia** → migraine typically has nausea and photo/phonophobia; TTH may have only one associated symptom (mild sensitivity)
- **Working long hours at a computer** → trigger: sustained poor posture, stress, muscle fatigue
- **Neck stiffness** → pericranial muscle tenderness is common
- **Normal neuro exam and vitals** → no red flags (e.g., papilledema, focal deficits)
- **Frequency 2-3 times per month** → consistent with episodic TTH (≥1/month but <15/month)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Chronic daily headache pattern:** Patient reports headache present >15 days/month for >3 months, with some days meeting migraine criteria (chronic TTH with migraine overlap). Be careful not to miss medication-overuse headache.
2. **Unilateral tension headache:** Rarely, TTH can be unilateral (∼10% of cases), mimicking migraine or cervicogenic headache. Key clue: still pressing/tightening quality and no typical migraine features.
3. **Tension headache with mild nausea:** Some patients report "queasiness" but not frank vomiting; this blurs the line with migraine. Look for other migraine features (e.g., throbbing, aura, aggravation by routine activity). If uncertain, treat as probable migraine initially.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Tension-type headache (TTH) is the most common primary headache disorder, characterized by mild-to-moderate bilateral head pain with a pressing/tightening quality, often described as a "tight band" around the head. It arises from a combination of peripheral muscle tension and central pain processing abnormalities, not from a structural lesion.

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Lifetime prevalence ~78% in the general population. It is the **most common headache** seen in primary care and on wards (though ward patients often have secondary headaches).
- **Stakes:** While benign, it causes significant disability and work absenteeism. The key pitfall is **misdiagnosing secondary headaches** (e.g., subarachnoid hemorrhage, meningitis, giant cell arteritis) as TTH. Also, overuse of over-the-counter analgesics can lead to medication-overuse headache.
- **Common pitfalls:** Failing to differentiate from migraine (especially when mild migraine has no nausea), dismissing high-risk features (e.g., late-onset headache, worst-ever headache), and treating with opioids or triptans (ineffective for TTH).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of development:**
1. **Peripheral trigger:** Sustained contraction of pericranial muscles (trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, temporalis) due to poor posture, stress, or prolonged screen time → microcirculation compromise → ischemia → release of inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, bradykinin, substance P).
2. **Muscle nociception:** These mediators activate Aδ and C fibers in muscle, generating pain signals that travel via trigeminal (V1–V3) and upper cervical (C1–C3) afferents to the trigeminocervical complex (TCC) in the brainstem.
3. **Central sensitization:** Repeated or sustained input leads to hyperexcitability of TCC neurons and loss of descending inhibitory control from the periaqueductal gray (PAG) and rostral ventromedial medulla (RVM). This explains why pain can persist even without ongoing muscle spasm (chronic TTH).
4. **No cortical spreading depression** (unlike migraine) → no aura, no significant vasodilation → explains lack of throbbing quality.

**Key clinical sign mechanism:**
- **Bilateral pressing pain:** Caused by bilateral pericranial muscle tension and central sensitization; the "band-like" quality is due to compression of muscle and fascia, not vascular pulsation.
- **No nausea/vomiting:** Absence of hypothalamic/vestibular activation (migraine involves brainstem nuclei that trigger nausea).
- **Pericranial muscle tenderness on palpation:** Directly reflects the peripheral source of nociception. Palpation of trapezius, temporalis, or suboccipital muscles reproduces pain.
- **No aggravation by routine activity:** Migraine is aggravated by walking stairs; TTH is not, because central sensitization is less intense and does not involve intracranial vasodilation.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern cues:**
- **Mid-afternoon onset** → cumulative muscle tension after hours of work
- **Neck stiffness** → upper trapezius and suboccipital muscle spasm
- **Improvement with rest/exercise** → relaxation reduces peripheral input, and descending inhibitory pathways can be activated by exercise
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause/Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (most common)** | No identifiable cause – primary headache | “I get these headaches when I’m stressed.” |
| **Myofascial / Postural** | Poor ergonomics, prolonged screen time, forward head posture | “I work at a desk 8 hours without breaks.” |
| **Psychosocial** | Stress, anxiety, depression, sleep deprivation | “I’ve been under a lot of pressure lately.” |
| **Cervical spine** | Cervical spondylosis, whiplash injury, muscle spasm from arthritis | “My neck is stiff, and the headache starts from the back.” |
| **Medication-related** | Overuse of analgesic (NSAIDs/paracetamol >10-15 days/month) → medication-overuse headache (MOH) | “I take painkillers for my headaches almost every day.” |
| **Temporomandibular joint (TMJ)** | Bruxism (teeth grinding), malocclusion, clenching | “My jaw clicks and I grind my teeth at night.” |
| **Caffeine withdrawal** | Abrupt cessation of >200 mg/day caffeine | “I stopped drinking coffee 2 days ago.” |
| **Endocrine** | Premenstrual syndrome, perimenopause (hormonal fluctuation) | “It happens the week before my period.” |

**Highlight most common cause:** **Idiopathic TTH** triggered by psychosocial stress and poor posture – accounts for >80% of episodic cases.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (ICHD-3):** Tension-type headache
- A. At least 10 episodes fulfilling B–D
- B. Headache lasting 30 min–7 days (episodic); ≥15 days/month for >3 months (chronic)
- C. At least two of: 1. bilateral; 2. pressing/tightening (non-pulsating); 3. mild-moderate; 4. not aggravated by routine activity
- D. Both of: 1. no nausea/vomiting (anorexia may occur); 2. no more than one of photophobia or phonophobia
- E. Not attributed to another disorder

**Tests (no routine tests required for classic TTH; order only if red flags):**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **None (clinical diagnosis)** | – | Only if classic criteria met and no red flags | Confirms TTH |
| **CT head (non-contrast)** | Space-occupying lesion, hemorrhage, hydrocephalus | Red flags: "worst ever", thunderclap, focal neuro deficit, new onset >50 years, immunosuppressed | Normal = TTH; abnormal = secondary headache |
| **ESR/CRP** | Elevated in giant cell arteritis, infection, inflammatory conditions | Age >50, new type of headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, polymyalgia rheumatica | ESR >50 mm/hr + headache → urgent prednisolone |
| **Lumbar puncture (if suspicion of SAH/meningitis)** | Opening pressure, WBC, protein, glucose, xanthochromia | "Worst ever", meningismus, fever, photophobia with fever | Xanthochromia = SAH; high WBC/low glucose = meningitis |
| **Cervical spine X-ray/MRI** | Degenerative disease, disc herniation, instability | Trauma, focal neck pain, radiculopathy, "headache triggered by neck movement" (cervicogenic) | Abnormal may explain secondary headache |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms/rules out**
- **Confirms TTH:** Bilateral, pressing non-throbbing pain lasting 30min–7d, no nausea, no more than one of photophobia/phonophobia, after excluding secondary causes.
- **Rules out TTH as primary:** Any focal neurological sign, persistent unilateral headache, thunderclap onset, or fever/meningismus.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):** *If presenting acutely (e.g., ED or clinic)*
- **Confirm it's not a secondary headache** (vitals, neuro exam, red flag screen). If red flags: CT/LP.
- **Acute abortive therapy** (choose one):
  1. **Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO** (or 200-400 mg liquid gel) – first-line NSAID. Take with food. Avoid if GI bleed risk/renal impairment.
  2. **Paracetamol 1000 mg PO** – alternative if NSAIDs contraindicated.
  3. **Aspirin 500–1000 mg PO** – effective but GI and bleeding risk.
- If severe or unresponsive to first-line: consider **combination with caffeine** (e.g., excedrin: paracetamol 250 mg + aspirin 250 mg + caffeine 65 mg – but not widely available in UK; can use caffeine tablet + ibuprofen).
- **Non-pharmacologic:** Offer a quiet dark room, cold/warm compress to neck, gentle massage.

**Why:** NSAIDs/paracetamol block peripheral nociception at the muscle level; caffeine enhances analgesia. Avoid opioids (risk of MOH, poor efficacy) and triptans (ineffective for TTH – no vasodilation to reverse).

**Ward (24–72h):** *If patient is hospitalized for other reasons with frequent TTH*
- **Optimize acute treatment timing** – take medication at onset of pain, not after it becomes severe.
- **Physical therapy** – refer to physiotherapy for postural correction, trigger point release, cervical mobilization.
- **Biofeedback / relaxation techniques** – progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, guided imagery.
- **Address triggers** – screen for sleep hygiene, stress management, ergonomics (adjust monitor height, take breaks every 30 min).
- **Consider short course of muscle relaxant** if severe muscle spasm: **Tizanidine 2 mg PO** (max 8 mg TID) or **Baclofen 5 mg PO** (max 20 mg TID) – but use cautiously for <1 week due to sedation.

**Long-term:**
- **Episodic TTH (>1 but <15 days/month):** No daily prophylaxis needed. Focus on trigger avoidance, acute treatment, and lifestyle modification.
- **Chronic TTH (≥15 days/month for >3 months):** Prophylaxis indicated.
  - **First-line:** **Amitriptyline** 10–25 mg PO QHS (start 10 mg, increase by 10 mg every 2 weeks up to 75 mg if needed). Takes 4–6 weeks to see benefit.
  - **Alternative:** **Mirtazapine** 15–30 mg PO QHS (if amitriptyline not tolerated), **Venlafaxine** 75–150 mg PO daily, or **Topiramate** 50–100 mg PO daily (but more side effects).
  - **Non-pharm:** Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), biofeedback, exercise program (aerobic 3x/week), acupuncture (moderate evidence).
- **Medication-overuse headache management:** Stop all acute analgesics for 1 month ("washout period"); bridge with amitriptyline if needed. Provide education that headaches will increase transiently then improve.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT prescribe triptans or ergotamines – they are ineffective for pure TTH.
- Do NOT prescribe long-term daily NSAIDs/paracetamol – risk of MOH, renal/GI toxicity.
- Do NOT refer to neurology for simple episodic TTH unless red flags appear or treatment fails.
- Do NOT order imaging for a typical TTH presentation – wasteful and exposes to radiation.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Tension Headache | Migraine without Aura | Cervicogenic Headache | Medication-Overuse Headache |
|---------|------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|----------------------------|
| Pain quality | Pressing/tightening, non-pulsatile | Throbbing/pulsatile, moderate-severe | Unilateral, starting from neck, often aching | Variable, often migrainous or TTH-like, daily |
| Location | Bilateral, band-like, holocranial | Unilateral (60%) or bilateral | Unilateral, occiput → forehead | Bilateral or unilateral |
| Duration | 30 min–7 days | 4–72 hours | Episodic or constant | Daily or near-daily |
| Associated symptoms | None or mild photo/phonophobia (only one) | Nausea/vomiting, photo & phonophobia, aura | Neck pain, restricted range of motion, referred pain | Withdrawal symptoms (morning headache, nausea) |
| Exam findings | Pericranial muscle tenderness | None or focal signs (if aura) | Cervical spine tenderness, positive Spurling's test | Normal |
| Aggravating factors | Stress, poor posture, fatigue | Menstruation, skipped meals, flickering lights, sleep disturbance | Neck movements, sustained posture | Analgesic withdrawal (morning) |
| Response to triptans | No/poor | Good | No/poor | May temporarily relieve but worsens cycle |
| Key differentiating clue | “Tight band”, no nausea, no activity aggravation | Throbbing, nausea, aggravation by routine activity | Headache start after neck injury/movement, unilateral with neck pain | Daily headache with use of analgesics >10–15 days/month |

- **Red flag diagnoses to always exclude:** Subarachnoid hemorrhage (thunderclap onset), meningitis (fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental status), giant cell arteritis (age >50, ESR >50, scalp tenderness), cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (headache + seizure/focal signs/risk factors), intracranial mass (worse in morning, vomiting, papilledema, progressive).
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications of underdiagnosing TTH are not from TTH itself, but from missing secondary causes.** However, chronic TTH leads to:

1. **Medication-overuse headache** – if patient treats repeated episodes with frequent analgesics. Presents as daily or near-daily headache that is often worse in the morning (withdrawal). Key signs: use of simple analgesics >15 days/month or combination analgesics >10 days/month for >3 months. Management: stop all acute medication, initiate prophylaxis, expect worsening for 2 weeks.
2. **Chronic tension-type headache transformation** – from episodic to chronic (≥15 headache days/month). This can lead to disability, depression, and poor quality of life. Prevention: timely recognition and lifestyle intervention.
3. **Unnecessary invasive testing** – misdiagnosing migraine as TTH and not ordering appropriate tests (e.g., for new daily persistent headache) can delay diagnosis of intracranial hypotension or IIH.
4. **Delayed diagnosis of serious secondary headache** – e.g., a patient with giant cell arteritis (GCA) presenting with headache and jaw claudication can be dismissed as TTH, leading to vision loss. Always screen for GCA in age >50: ESR/CRP, temporal artery biopsy.

**Late-presentation patterns:**
- **If untreated:** The headache resolves spontaneously (episodic TTH resolves within hours to days) but may recur frequently. Chronic TTH persists but does not cause structural damage.
- **The “big mistake” stem:** “A 60-year-old with new onset headache, temporal tenderness, ESR 80, thought to have tension headache → started NSAIDs → vision loss.” Key lesson: **always think GCA in older patients with new headache.**
- **Another trap:** “Young woman with daily headaches taking ibuprofen 5 times a week → doctor increases dose → headache worsens → CT normal → finally diagnosed with MOH.” Key lesson: **ask about OTC medication use.**
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for ICHD-3 diagnostic criteria of TTH:**
- **T** – Tightness (pressing/tightening)
- **E** – Episodic (or chronic)
- **N** – No nausea
- **S** – Sensitivity (only one of photo/phonophobia)
- **I** – Intensity mild-moderate
- **O** – Opposite of activity aggravation (not aggravated)
- **N** – Normal exam

Alternatively: **“THIN”** – Tightness, Headache bilateral, Intensity mild-moderate, No nausea (plus no more than one photophobia/phonophobia).

**5–7 Clinical Pearls:**
1. **The “rule of 2/4”:** For a positive diagnosis of TTH, you need at least 2 of 4 features (bilateral, pressing, mild-mod, not aggravated by activity) **AND** no nausea and no more than one of photo/phonophobia.
2. **Palpate the muscles:** In clinic, gently palpate temporalis, trapezius, and suboccipital muscles. Pain reproduction strongly supports TTH.
3. **Always ask about analgesic use** in any frequent headache – you will miss MOH if you don't.
4. **Amitriptyline is first-line prophylaxis, not acute treatment.** It takes weeks to work; never use it as an abortive.
5. **Headache diary is gold:** Ask the patient to record frequency, severity, triggers, and medication use for 4 weeks to guide management.
6. **Tension headache does NOT cause throbbing pain.** If patient says “pounding”, think migraine first.
7. **Neck stiffness ≠ meningitis.** In TTH, neck stiffness is due to muscle spasm and improves with massage; in meningitis, it's true nuchal rigidity (unable to touch chin to chest, Kernig/Brudzinski positive).

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **Skipping red flag screen** – always perform a focused neuro exam, fundoscopy, and check for nuchal rigidity.
- **Over-reliance on imaging** – imaging is not needed for typical TTH; it increases false positives and radiation exposure.
- **Using triptans** – they are ineffective and expensive in TTH.
- **Forgetting to stop overused medication** – if patient has MOH, giving more analgesics will worsen the headache.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team?**

| Situation | Action | Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| New focal neurological deficit (hemiparesis, aphasia, visual field cut) | Urgent CT head + neurology consult | Any new deficit |
| Thunderclap headache (onset <1 min) | CT head ASAP; if normal → LP for xanthochromia | Suspect SAH |
| Headache with fever + nuchal rigidity + altered mental status | ED review; CT head (if indicated) then LP | Suspect meningitis/encephalitis |
| Headache in immunocompromised (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy) | Senior review; CT+LP to rule out opportunistic infection | Any new headache |
| Headache with papilledema + vomiting | Urgent CT head; measure opening pressure on LP | Suspect raised ICP / IIH |
| GCS <13 or deteriorating | Crash team / ICU | GCS ≤12 |
| NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L with headache | Senior review; consider sepsis | Not typical simple TTH |

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2:** Score 0-2 for uncomplicated TTH. Score ≥5 or any single parameter at red level (e.g., RR ≤8 or ≥25, O2 sat ≤91%, SBP ≤90) requires escalation.
- **Lactate:** >2 mmol/L with headache suggests possible metabolic cause or sepsis – not TTH.
- **GCS:** <15 in headache patient is a red flag; <13 → urgent CT/ICU consult.
- **Intracranial pressure:** If papilledema present, formal visual field testing and neuro-ophthalmology referral within 24 hours.

**When to call a senior (registrar/consultant) for management advice:**
- Failure of two first-line acute treatments within 2 hours
- Suspected MOH (need guidance on washout protocol)
- Need for prophylactic medication in chronic TTH (especially if comorbid psychiatric condition)
- Diagnostic uncertainty after red flag work-up ruled out secondary causes

**Remember:** Tension headache is a diagnosis of exclusion – if any red flag appears, escalate appropriately. The junior doctor's role is to know the typical picture, but also to know when to **act like a senior** and call for help.

rawMarkdown:
## Tension Headache
**One-liner:** A bilateral, pressing/tightening headache of mild-moderate intensity, not worsened by routine activity, caused by pericranial muscle tension and central sensitization.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:** A 32-year-old female marketing executive presents with a bifrontal "band-like" headache that started mid-afternoon. She describes it as a constant pressure, not throbbing. No nausea, no photophobia, no phonophobia (or only one of these). She has been working long hours at a computer, and her neck feels stiff. Neurological exam is normal, and vital signs are stable. She reports similar headaches 2-3 times per month.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Bifrontal, "band-like" pain** → hallmark of tension-type headache (TTH); bilateral, pressing quality
- **Mid-afternoon onset** → often builds slowly as the day progresses, reflecting muscle tension accumulation
- **Constant pressure, not throbbing** → distinguishes from migraine (which is often pulsatile)
- **No nausea, no photophobia/phonophobia** → migraine typically has nausea and photo/phonophobia; TTH may have only one associated symptom (mild sensitivity)
- **Working long hours at a computer** → trigger: sustained poor posture, stress, muscle fatigue
- **Neck stiffness** → pericranial muscle tenderness is common
- **Normal neuro exam and vitals** → no red flags (e.g., papilledema, focal deficits)
- **Frequency 2-3 times per month** → consistent with episodic TTH (≥1/month but <15/month)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Chronic daily headache pattern:** Patient reports headache present >15 days/month for >3 months, with some days meeting migraine criteria (chronic TTH with migraine overlap). Be careful not to miss medication-overuse headache.
2. **Unilateral tension headache:** Rarely, TTH can be unilateral (∼10% of cases), mimicking migraine or cervicogenic headache. Key clue: still pressing/tightening quality and no typical migraine features.
3. **Tension headache with mild nausea:** Some patients report "queasiness" but not frank vomiting; this blurs the line with migraine. Look for other migraine features (e.g., throbbing, aura, aggravation by routine activity). If uncertain, treat as probable migraine initially.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:** Tension-type headache (TTH) is the most common primary headache disorder, characterized by mild-to-moderate bilateral head pain with a pressing/tightening quality, often described as a "tight band" around the head. It arises from a combination of peripheral muscle tension and central pain processing abnormalities, not from a structural lesion.

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Lifetime prevalence ~78% in the general population. It is the **most common headache** seen in primary care and on wards (though ward patients often have secondary headaches).
- **Stakes:** While benign, it causes significant disability and work absenteeism. The key pitfall is **misdiagnosing secondary headaches** (e.g., subarachnoid hemorrhage, meningitis, giant cell arteritis) as TTH. Also, overuse of over-the-counter analgesics can lead to medication-overuse headache.
- **Common pitfalls:** Failing to differentiate from migraine (especially when mild migraine has no nausea), dismissing high-risk features (e.g., late-onset headache, worst-ever headache), and treating with opioids or triptans (ineffective for TTH).

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**Story of development:**
1. **Peripheral trigger:** Sustained contraction of pericranial muscles (trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, temporalis) due to poor posture, stress, or prolonged screen time → microcirculation compromise → ischemia → release of inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, bradykinin, substance P).
2. **Muscle nociception:** These mediators activate Aδ and C fibers in muscle, generating pain signals that travel via trigeminal (V1–V3) and upper cervical (C1–C3) afferents to the trigeminocervical complex (TCC) in the brainstem.
3. **Central sensitization:** Repeated or sustained input leads to hyperexcitability of TCC neurons and loss of descending inhibitory control from the periaqueductal gray (PAG) and rostral ventromedial medulla (RVM). This explains why pain can persist even without ongoing muscle spasm (chronic TTH).
4. **No cortical spreading depression** (unlike migraine) → no aura, no significant vasodilation → explains lack of throbbing quality.

**Key clinical sign mechanism:**
- **Bilateral pressing pain:** Caused by bilateral pericranial muscle tension and central sensitization; the "band-like" quality is due to compression of muscle and fascia, not vascular pulsation.
- **No nausea/vomiting:** Absence of hypothalamic/vestibular activation (migraine involves brainstem nuclei that trigger nausea).
- **Pericranial muscle tenderness on palpation:** Directly reflects the peripheral source of nociception. Palpation of trapezius, temporalis, or suboccipital muscles reproduces pain.
- **No aggravation by routine activity:** Migraine is aggravated by walking stairs; TTH is not, because central sensitization is less intense and does not involve intracranial vasodilation.

**Connecting pathophys to pattern cues:**
- **Mid-afternoon onset** → cumulative muscle tension after hours of work
- **Neck stiffness** → upper trapezius and suboccipital muscle spasm
- **Improvement with rest/exercise** → relaxation reduces peripheral input, and descending inhibitory pathways can be activated by exercise

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Cause/Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (most common)** | No identifiable cause – primary headache | “I get these headaches when I’m stressed.” |
| **Myofascial / Postural** | Poor ergonomics, prolonged screen time, forward head posture | “I work at a desk 8 hours without breaks.” |
| **Psychosocial** | Stress, anxiety, depression, sleep deprivation | “I’ve been under a lot of pressure lately.” |
| **Cervical spine** | Cervical spondylosis, whiplash injury, muscle spasm from arthritis | “My neck is stiff, and the headache starts from the back.” |
| **Medication-related** | Overuse of analgesic (NSAIDs/paracetamol >10-15 days/month) → medication-overuse headache (MOH) | “I take painkillers for my headaches almost every day.” |
| **Temporomandibular joint (TMJ)** | Bruxism (teeth grinding), malocclusion, clenching | “My jaw clicks and I grind my teeth at night.” |
| **Caffeine withdrawal** | Abrupt cessation of >200 mg/day caffeine | “I stopped drinking coffee 2 days ago.” |
| **Endocrine** | Premenstrual syndrome, perimenopause (hormonal fluctuation) | “It happens the week before my period.” |

**Highlight most common cause:** **Idiopathic TTH** triggered by psychosocial stress and poor posture – accounts for >80% of episodic cases.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria (ICHD-3):** Tension-type headache
- A. At least 10 episodes fulfilling B–D
- B. Headache lasting 30 min–7 days (episodic); ≥15 days/month for >3 months (chronic)
- C. At least two of: 1. bilateral; 2. pressing/tightening (non-pulsating); 3. mild-moderate; 4. not aggravated by routine activity
- D. Both of: 1. no nausea/vomiting (anorexia may occur); 2. no more than one of photophobia or phonophobia
- E. Not attributed to another disorder

**Tests (no routine tests required for classic TTH; order only if red flags):**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **None (clinical diagnosis)** | – | Only if classic criteria met and no red flags | Confirms TTH |
| **CT head (non-contrast)** | Space-occupying lesion, hemorrhage, hydrocephalus | Red flags: "worst ever", thunderclap, focal neuro deficit, new onset >50 years, immunosuppressed | Normal = TTH; abnormal = secondary headache |
| **ESR/CRP** | Elevated in giant cell arteritis, infection, inflammatory conditions | Age >50, new type of headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, polymyalgia rheumatica | ESR >50 mm/hr + headache → urgent prednisolone |
| **Lumbar puncture (if suspicion of SAH/meningitis)** | Opening pressure, WBC, protein, glucose, xanthochromia | "Worst ever", meningismus, fever, photophobia with fever | Xanthochromia = SAH; high WBC/low glucose = meningitis |
| **Cervical spine X-ray/MRI** | Degenerative disease, disc herniation, instability | Trauma, focal neck pain, radiculopathy, "headache triggered by neck movement" (cervicogenic) | Abnormal may explain secondary headache |

**Pattern-recognition: what single finding confirms/rules out**
- **Confirms TTH:** Bilateral, pressing non-throbbing pain lasting 30min–7d, no nausea, no more than one of photophobia/phonophobia, after excluding secondary causes.
- **Rules out TTH as primary:** Any focal neurological sign, persistent unilateral headache, thunderclap onset, or fever/meningismus.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour):** *If presenting acutely (e.g., ED or clinic)*
- **Confirm it's not a secondary headache** (vitals, neuro exam, red flag screen). If red flags: CT/LP.
- **Acute abortive therapy** (choose one):
  1. **Ibuprofen 400–600 mg PO** (or 200-400 mg liquid gel) – first-line NSAID. Take with food. Avoid if GI bleed risk/renal impairment.
  2. **Paracetamol 1000 mg PO** – alternative if NSAIDs contraindicated.
  3. **Aspirin 500–1000 mg PO** – effective but GI and bleeding risk.
- If severe or unresponsive to first-line: consider **combination with caffeine** (e.g., excedrin: paracetamol 250 mg + aspirin 250 mg + caffeine 65 mg – but not widely available in UK; can use caffeine tablet + ibuprofen).
- **Non-pharmacologic:** Offer a quiet dark room, cold/warm compress to neck, gentle massage.

**Why:** NSAIDs/paracetamol block peripheral nociception at the muscle level; caffeine enhances analgesia. Avoid opioids (risk of MOH, poor efficacy) and triptans (ineffective for TTH – no vasodilation to reverse).

**Ward (24–72h):** *If patient is hospitalized for other reasons with frequent TTH*
- **Optimize acute treatment timing** – take medication at onset of pain, not after it becomes severe.
- **Physical therapy** – refer to physiotherapy for postural correction, trigger point release, cervical mobilization.
- **Biofeedback / relaxation techniques** – progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, guided imagery.
- **Address triggers** – screen for sleep hygiene, stress management, ergonomics (adjust monitor height, take breaks every 30 min).
- **Consider short course of muscle relaxant** if severe muscle spasm: **Tizanidine 2 mg PO** (max 8 mg TID) or **Baclofen 5 mg PO** (max 20 mg TID) – but use cautiously for <1 week due to sedation.

**Long-term:**
- **Episodic TTH (>1 but <15 days/month):** No daily prophylaxis needed. Focus on trigger avoidance, acute treatment, and lifestyle modification.
- **Chronic TTH (≥15 days/month for >3 months):** Prophylaxis indicated.
  - **First-line:** **Amitriptyline** 10–25 mg PO QHS (start 10 mg, increase by 10 mg every 2 weeks up to 75 mg if needed). Takes 4–6 weeks to see benefit.
  - **Alternative:** **Mirtazapine** 15–30 mg PO QHS (if amitriptyline not tolerated), **Venlafaxine** 75–150 mg PO daily, or **Topiramate** 50–100 mg PO daily (but more side effects).
  - **Non-pharm:** Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), biofeedback, exercise program (aerobic 3x/week), acupuncture (moderate evidence).
- **Medication-overuse headache management:** Stop all acute analgesics for 1 month ("washout period"); bridge with amitriptyline if needed. Provide education that headaches will increase transiently then improve.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT prescribe triptans or ergotamines – they are ineffective for pure TTH.
- Do NOT prescribe long-term daily NSAIDs/paracetamol – risk of MOH, renal/GI toxicity.
- Do NOT refer to neurology for simple episodic TTH unless red flags appear or treatment fails.
- Do NOT order imaging for a typical TTH presentation – wasteful and exposes to radiation.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Tension Headache | Migraine without Aura | Cervicogenic Headache | Medication-Overuse Headache |
|---------|------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|----------------------------|
| Pain quality | Pressing/tightening, non-pulsatile | Throbbing/pulsatile, moderate-severe | Unilateral, starting from neck, often aching | Variable, often migrainous or TTH-like, daily |
| Location | Bilateral, band-like, holocranial | Unilateral (60%) or bilateral | Unilateral, occiput → forehead | Bilateral or unilateral |
| Duration | 30 min–7 days | 4–72 hours | Episodic or constant | Daily or near-daily |
| Associated symptoms | None or mild photo/phonophobia (only one) | Nausea/vomiting, photo & phonophobia, aura | Neck pain, restricted range of motion, referred pain | Withdrawal symptoms (morning headache, nausea) |
| Exam findings | Pericranial muscle tenderness | None or focal signs (if aura) | Cervical spine tenderness, positive Spurling's test | Normal |
| Aggravating factors | Stress, poor posture, fatigue | Menstruation, skipped meals, flickering lights, sleep disturbance | Neck movements, sustained posture | Analgesic withdrawal (morning) |
| Response to triptans | No/poor | Good | No/poor | May temporarily relieve but worsens cycle |
| Key differentiating clue | “Tight band”, no nausea, no activity aggravation | Throbbing, nausea, aggravation by routine activity | Headache start after neck injury/movement, unilateral with neck pain | Daily headache with use of analgesics >10–15 days/month |

- **Red flag diagnoses to always exclude:** Subarachnoid hemorrhage (thunderclap onset), meningitis (fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental status), giant cell arteritis (age >50, ESR >50, scalp tenderness), cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (headache + seizure/focal signs/risk factors), intracranial mass (worse in morning, vomiting, papilledema, progressive).

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
**Major complications of underdiagnosing TTH are not from TTH itself, but from missing secondary causes.** However, chronic TTH leads to:

1. **Medication-overuse headache** – if patient treats repeated episodes with frequent analgesics. Presents as daily or near-daily headache that is often worse in the morning (withdrawal). Key signs: use of simple analgesics >15 days/month or combination analgesics >10 days/month for >3 months. Management: stop all acute medication, initiate prophylaxis, expect worsening for 2 weeks.
2. **Chronic tension-type headache transformation** – from episodic to chronic (≥15 headache days/month). This can lead to disability, depression, and poor quality of life. Prevention: timely recognition and lifestyle intervention.
3. **Unnecessary invasive testing** – misdiagnosing migraine as TTH and not ordering appropriate tests (e.g., for new daily persistent headache) can delay diagnosis of intracranial hypotension or IIH.
4. **Delayed diagnosis of serious secondary headache** – e.g., a patient with giant cell arteritis (GCA) presenting with headache and jaw claudication can be dismissed as TTH, leading to vision loss. Always screen for GCA in age >50: ESR/CRP, temporal artery biopsy.

**Late-presentation patterns:**
- **If untreated:** The headache resolves spontaneously (episodic TTH resolves within hours to days) but may recur frequently. Chronic TTH persists but does not cause structural damage.
- **The “big mistake” stem:** “A 60-year-old with new onset headache, temporal tenderness, ESR 80, thought to have tension headache → started NSAIDs → vision loss.” Key lesson: **always think GCA in older patients with new headache.**
- **Another trap:** “Young woman with daily headaches taking ibuprofen 5 times a week → doctor increases dose → headache worsens → CT normal → finally diagnosed with MOH.” Key lesson: **ask about OTC medication use.**

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonic for ICHD-3 diagnostic criteria of TTH:**
- **T** – Tightness (pressing/tightening)
- **E** – Episodic (or chronic)
- **N** – No nausea
- **S** – Sensitivity (only one of photo/phonophobia)
- **I** – Intensity mild-moderate
- **O** – Opposite of activity aggravation (not aggravated)
- **N** – Normal exam

Alternatively: **“THIN”** – Tightness, Headache bilateral, Intensity mild-moderate, No nausea (plus no more than one photophobia/phonophobia).

**5–7 Clinical Pearls:**
1. **The “rule of 2/4”:** For a positive diagnosis of TTH, you need at least 2 of 4 features (bilateral, pressing, mild-mod, not aggravated by activity) **AND** no nausea and no more than one of photo/phonophobia.
2. **Palpate the muscles:** In clinic, gently palpate temporalis, trapezius, and suboccipital muscles. Pain reproduction strongly supports TTH.
3. **Always ask about analgesic use** in any frequent headache – you will miss MOH if you don't.
4. **Amitriptyline is first-line prophylaxis, not acute treatment.** It takes weeks to work; never use it as an abortive.
5. **Headache diary is gold:** Ask the patient to record frequency, severity, triggers, and medication use for 4 weeks to guide management.
6. **Tension headache does NOT cause throbbing pain.** If patient says “pounding”, think migraine first.
7. **Neck stiffness ≠ meningitis.** In TTH, neck stiffness is due to muscle spasm and improves with massage; in meningitis, it's true nuchal rigidity (unable to touch chin to chest, Kernig/Brudzinski positive).

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **Skipping red flag screen** – always perform a focused neuro exam, fundoscopy, and check for nuchal rigidity.
- **Over-reliance on imaging** – imaging is not needed for typical TTH; it increases false positives and radiation exposure.
- **Using triptans** – they are ineffective and expensive in TTH.
- **Forgetting to stop overused medication** – if patient has MOH, giving more analgesics will worsen the headache.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team?**

| Situation | Action | Threshold |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| New focal neurological deficit (hemiparesis, aphasia, visual field cut) | Urgent CT head + neurology consult | Any new deficit |
| Thunderclap headache (onset <1 min) | CT head ASAP; if normal → LP for xanthochromia | Suspect SAH |
| Headache with fever + nuchal rigidity + altered mental status | ED review; CT head (if indicated) then LP | Suspect meningitis/encephalitis |
| Headache in immunocompromised (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy) | Senior review; CT+LP to rule out opportunistic infection | Any new headache |
| Headache with papilledema + vomiting | Urgent CT head; measure opening pressure on LP | Suspect raised ICP / IIH |
| GCS <13 or deteriorating | Crash team / ICU | GCS ≤12 |
| NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L with headache | Senior review; consider sepsis | Not typical simple TTH |

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2:** Score 0-2 for uncomplicated TTH. Score ≥5 or any single parameter at red level (e.g., RR ≤8 or ≥25, O2 sat ≤91%, SBP ≤90) requires escalation.
- **Lactate:** >2 mmol/L with headache suggests possible metabolic cause or sepsis – not TTH.
- **GCS:** <15 in headache patient is a red flag; <13 → urgent CT/ICU consult.
- **Intracranial pressure:** If papilledema present, formal visual field testing and neuro-ophthalmology referral within 24 hours.

**When to call a senior (registrar/consultant) for management advice:**
- Failure of two first-line acute treatments within 2 hours
- Suspected MOH (need guidance on washout protocol)
- Need for prophylactic medication in chronic TTH (especially if comorbid psychiatric condition)
- Diagnostic uncertainty after red flag work-up ruled out secondary causes

**Remember:** Tension headache is a diagnosis of exclusion – if any red flag appears, escalate appropriately. The junior doctor's role is to know the typical picture, but also to know when to **act like a senior** and call for help.
